sections.title: Asking Questions
sections.explanation: There are three ways to ask a yes/no question in French. (1) Intonation: keep the word order the same as a statement and simply raise your voice at the end — 'Tu parles français?' This is the most common in everyday speech. (2) Est-ce que: place 'est-ce que' (or 'est-ce qu'' before a vowel) at the start of the sentence — 'Est-ce que tu parles français?' This works in all registers. (3) Inversion: swap the subject pronoun and verb, joined by a hyphen — 'Parles-tu français?' This is formal and mostly written. When the verb ends in a vowel and the subject is il, elle, or on, add -t- between them: 'Parle-t-il français?'. For information questions, use question words (mots interrogatifs): où (where), quand (when), comment (how), combien de (how many/much), pourquoi (why), qui (who), que / qu'est-ce que (what), quel/quelle (which/what).
sections.tip: Est-ce que is the safest choice — it works in speaking and writing at any level. Inversion is expected in formal writing and on exams. Intonation alone is very natural in conversation but avoid it in writing.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a correct yes/no question using est-ce que?
Which inversion form is correct for 'elle parle'?
What does the question word 'où' mean?
___ tu t'appelles? (What is your name? — using comment)
___ est-ce que tu pars? (When are you leaving?)
How do you turn 'Il mange' into an inversion question?
___ est-ce que tu fais? (What are you doing?)

sections.title: Making Sentences Negative
sections.explanation: To make a sentence negative in French, wrap the verb with 'ne' before it and 'pas' after it: 'Je parle' → 'Je ne parle pas' (I don't speak). Before a vowel or silent h, 'ne' shortens to 'n'': 'J'ai' → 'Je n'ai pas' (I don't have). In spoken French, the 'ne' is very often dropped informally: 'Je parle pas', 'Il mange pas'. Besides ne...pas, there are other negative expressions: ne...jamais (never), ne...rien (nothing), ne...plus (no longer / not anymore), ne...personne (nobody), ne...que (only). An important rule: after any negation, the articles du, de la, des, un, and une all change to 'de' (or 'd'' before a vowel). For example: 'J'ai du pain' → 'Je n'ai pas de pain'; 'Elle a une voiture' → 'Elle n'a pas de voiture'.
sections.tip: Remember the article change after negation — du, de la, des, un, une all become 'de'. The only exception is with the verb être: 'Ce n'est pas un problème' keeps 'un'.

examples.fr: Il ne mange jamais de viande.
Il ne boit que de l'eau.

examples.en: I don't have any bread. (du → de after negation)

exercises.prompt: How do you make 'Je mange' negative?
Je n'___ pas de voiture. (I don't have a car.)
What happens to 'du' after negation? 'J'ai du café' → 'Je n'ai pas ___ café.'
Which sentence means 'She no longer lives here'?
Which is the correct negative of 'Elle a une amie'?
What does 'Je ne parle que français' mean?
options: I don't speak French at all.
